meta.purpose: Terminology boundaries, traps, and self-checks for Web Animation Performance.
meta.load_condition: Load with Core for any generation, review, or refactor task in this domain.

standard_terms.definition: A visible frame drop caused by animation work exceeding the frame budget, resulting in stutter or uneven motion.
Animation that uses exclusively transform, opacity, and hardware-accelerated filter properties, avoiding layout and paint triggers.
A cascade of paint-only style changes within a single frame that exceed the raster budget.
The strict time ceiling per rendered frame (~16.7ms at 60fps), shared by script, layout, paint, and composite phases.

banned_terms.why: will-change consumes GPU memory per element. Overuse degrades performance globally by exhausting compositor memory and triggering layer explosion.
banned_terms.replace_with: Replace with specific criteria from the domain ontology.

banned_terms.why: requestAnimationFrame is the default animation loop — adding it does not fix rendering. Diagnose the actual bottleneck first.
banned_terms.replace_with: Replace with specific criteria from the domain ontology.

banned_terms.why: Vague term that often leads to translateZ(0) hacks without understanding compositor layer cost. Use measurable criteria instead.
banned_terms.replace_with: Replace with specific criteria from the domain ontology.

misunderstandings.wrong: If it runs smoothly on a developer machine, performance is fine.
misunderstandings.correct: Developer machines often have 4-8x the GPU power of mid-range mobile devices. Test on the target device tier or use CPU/GPU throttling.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Developer machines often have 4-8x the GPU power of mid-range mobile devices. Test on the target device tier or use CPU/GPU throttling.
misunderstandings.why: Holding the wrong interpretation leads to poor judgment: Developer machines often have 4-8x the GPU power of mid-range mobile devices. Test on the target device tier or use CPU/GPU throttling.

misunderstandings.wrong: Reducing animated elements always improves performance.
misunderstandings.correct: One element animating width can cost more than 50 elements animating transform. The animated property matters more than the element count.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: One element animating width can cost more than 50 elements animating transform. The animated property matters more than the element count.
misunderstandings.why: Holding the wrong interpretation leads to poor judgment: One element animating width can cost more than 50 elements animating transform. The animated property matters more than the element count.

self_check: Do all animations that run continuously use only compositor-only properties (transform, opacity)?
Does every animation respect prefers-reduced-motion with a functional alternative?
Was this animation profiled on a throttled CPU/GPU or representative low-end device?
Does the animation fit within the defined frame budget when profiled?
